context: Current closing price of Altria (MO): $65.91 USD as of 2026-04-08.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a mild mean-reversion assumption anchored around a current price level I estimated near $65-66. At the one-day horizon, I expected very little drift from the prevailing price, so the estimate of $65.75 essentially reflects the last known price with a slight adjustment for typical overnight or intraday momentum. At the one-week horizon, I trimmed the estimate marginally to $65.50, acknowledging that short-term price pressure or profit-taking could nudge the asset slightly lower, while not expecting any dramatic directional move. At the one-month horizon, the estimate of $65.00 reflects a somewhat more conservative view, incorporating the general tendency of prices to revert toward medium-term averages and the slightly greater probability of adverse macro developments accumulating over a longer window.

The confidence interval widths were calibrated primarily around historical volatility. At one day, the tight band of roughly $63.80 to $67.70 reflects that daily price swings in this asset class tend to be modest under normal conditions. As the horizon extends to one week, the interval widens to account for the compounding of daily moves, potential scheduled data releases, and the possibility of sentiment shifts. By one month, the interval spans roughly $59 to $71.50, which captures the realistic range of outcomes given that macro themes, earnings cycles, or policy changes can accumulate meaningfully over several weeks.

Several specific risks could push outcomes outside these intervals. On the downside, a sudden deterioration in risk appetite — driven by central bank hawkishness, geopolitical escalation, or a negative macro surprise — could easily breach the lower bound at any horizon. Liquidity stress or a correlated sell-off in related assets could amplify moves beyond what historical volatility alone would suggest. On the upside, a stronger-than-expected economic print, a dovish policy pivot, or a short-squeeze dynamic could push prices well above the upper bound, particularly at the one-week and one-month horizons where there is more time for such catalysts to develop.

It is also worth noting that my intervals were deliberately set at 90% confidence, meaning roughly one in ten outcomes should fall outside them by construction. Model uncertainty itself — the possibility that the underlying data generating process shifts — is a further reason to treat these intervals as reasonable guides rather than hard boundaries. Black swan events, by definition, are not well-captured by extrapolating recent statistical patterns, and any interval built on historical data will understate tail risks in genuinely novel market regimes.
